original_hypothesis.title: Phishing Theft of ETH_RUNE via tx.origin Authorization
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The ETH_RUNE contract contains a non-standard transferTo function that uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender to determine the source of funds. An attacker can deploy a malicious contract that, when interacted with by a user, calls ETH_RUNE.transferTo(attacker, userBalance), stealing the tokens without approval.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Using tx.origin for authorization is a well-known critical vulnerability. If users still hold ETH_RUNE, they are at severe risk of phishing attacks.
preconditions: User is tricked into interacting with a malicious contract

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Bypasses allowance checks entirely by using tx.origin
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious contract intercepts the transaction flow to drain funds
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker steals 100% of the victim's ETH_RUNE balance

economic_questions: Is ETH_RUNE still actively traded or held by users?
Has the upgrade to native RUNE been completed?
validation_requirements: Verify ETH_RUNE holder distribution on mainnet
Confirm the vulnerability is exploitable via standard phishing patterns

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
